title-group.article-title: Genetic evidence for causal relationship between general cognition and treatment resistance in schizophrenia

abstract.p: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS) is characterized by symptoms unresponsive to standard antipsychotics, possibly representing a distinct subtype of schizophrenia with unique neurobiological mechanisms. TRS may share neurodevelopmental mechanisms with cognitive impairment, which is also frequently resistant to treatment. However, the association between TRS and cognitive impairment remains inconclusive in the literature. This study aims to investigate the genetic associations between TRS and general cognitive function, operationalized by general intelligence (GF) and educational attainment (years of education [EAY] and college completion [CC]), by analyzing genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics using Two-sample Mendelian Randomization (MR) methodology. We also employed conditional false discovery rate (cFDR) analyses to increase statistical power to identify genetic associations for TRS, leveraging on association results for GF and EAY. Negative genetic association between general cognition and treatment resistance in schizophrenia was revealed by MR results. Four previously unreported TRS risk loci conditioning on general cognition at cFDR <0.01 were identified. The shared credible genes mapped to loci was TMX1. The gene sets enriched with identified shared genes, including GOCC_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX, GOCC_NLRP3_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX, and GOMF_CASPASE_BINDING gene sets, were related to inflammatory processes. These results provide evidence on the possible causative link between general cognition or a closely correlated trait and TRS, and the presence of complex and unique biological mechanisms in the development of treatment-resistant schizophrenia.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Even with adequate trials of antipsychotic medication, about 15–30% of patients with schizophrenia continue to experience persistent psychotic symptoms and are considered to have treatment resistant schizophrenia (TRS) [1–3]. Patients with TRS have poorer functional recovery including higher rates of unemployment, worse quality of life [4] and more physical health comorbidities [5], and incur 3–11 fold higher direct healthcare costs than the schizophrenia population in general [6]. Clozapine is the only antipsychotic medication with documented efficacy for TRS [7]. However, about 30–70% of TRS patients have inadequate response to clozapine [8] with limited effective treatment [9]. Therefore, understanding the nature and mechanisms of TRS would be crucial for the development of effective personalized interventional strategies.

Both presynaptic dopaminergic dysfunction [10] and NMDA receptor hypofunction [11, 12] have been proposed as possible underlying neurobiological mechanisms for schizophrenia, supported by meta-analyses of studies using positron emission tomography (PET), single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) [13], or 1H magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-MRS) [11]. Furthermore, compared to other patients with schizophrenia, patients with TRS have lower dopamine synthesis capacity [14], higher glutamate levels in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) [15], and more cortical thinning in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex [16]. These results suggest that TRS may be a subtype of schizophrenia with distinct neurobiological mechanisms. On the other hand, a meta-analysis has found that patients with TRS had a wider range of neurocognitive deficits compared with those responding to treatment, suggesting that patients with TRS may represent the severe end of a continuum of neurodevelopmental liability associated with schizophrenia [17]. Moreover, younger age of onset and poor premorbid functioning have frequently been reported to be a predictor of TRS [2, 18], further supporting the neurodevelopmental nature of the TRS mechanism. The median time of identification of TRS is about 10 years after the illness onset [19], and a clinical course characterized by frequent relapse is a strong predictor for TRS [2, 20]. This evidence, along with the dopamine supersensitivity hypothesis of TRS development [21], indicates a neuro-progressive nature of the mechanisms of TRS development. Therefore, the poorer cognitive functions in TRS patients could also be part of an accelerated neurodegenerative process.

A possible genetic association between cognitive deficit and schizophrenia has been demonstrated by the presence of cognitive deficits in unaffected relatives of individuals with schizophrenia [22], as well as the familial correlation between schizophrenia and poor cognitive functions in twins and families of patients [23, 24]. Genome-wide association studies (GWASs) also provide evidence on the link between the genetic architectures of schizophrenia and many cognitive qualities known to be compromised in schizophrenia, such as general cognitive function [23, 24]. A Previous polygenic score analysis [22] suggested that over one-third of variation in schizophrenia polygenic score was influenced by genes related to cognition pathways. However, studies on genetic contribution to the TRS have been limited and results are inconsistent. Existing evidence from a review [25] shows that TRS and clozapine resistance are associated with genetic factors mainly related to dopaminergic and serotoninergic systems, including TRS with rs4680 and rs4818 in COMT and rs1799978 in DRD2, clozapine resistance with DRD3 and CYP1A2 polymorphisms, and weight gain with LEP and SNAP-25 genes. Larger multi-site studies are needed to clarify risk genes, gene interactions, and their links to clinical factors in TRS and clozapine resistance. Furthermore, three studies reported higher polygenic risk scores of schizophrenia for TRS compared with non-TRS [26–28], however, others did not replicate this finding [29, 30]. A recent GWAS study indicated a distinct polygenic contribution to treatment resistance, separate from general schizophrenia liability and revealed intelligence and cognition are genetically correlated with TRS despite overlapping samples that may introduce partially dependent p-values [31]. However, the nature of the relationship of cognition with TRS status is unclear. Under the neurodevelopmental continuum hypothesis [32], cognitive impairment, schizophrenia, and other neurodevelopmental disorders are pleiotropic outcomes of the same genetic risk, suggesting shared genetic basis and neurodevelopmental mechanisms for TRS and cognition.

Mendelian randomization (MR) is an analytic approach to examine the causality of an observed risk factor and a clinical outcome [33]. Utilizing publicly available GWAS data, an observed association between the genetic instrumental variable and outcomes in the MR analysis can provide support to the hypothesis that the exposure in question is causally related to the outcomes. So far, only two studies have utilised the MR approach to examine possible causal risk factors of TRS [34, 35], and none included cognitive function. The current study thus aims to use MR methods to explore the possible genetic association between TRS and general cognitive performance (intelligence and education attainment). To increase statistical power for genetic discovery, the conditional false discovery rate (cFDR) paradigm is used [36, 37] in the latest TRS GWAS [31] to understand the specific genetic contribution of TRS. Given evidence of the genetic association between schizophrenia and cognition, we hypothesized that TRS status and general cognition may have a shared genetic basis, and likely to involve both neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative processes. The cFDR framework will be used to explore previously unreported risk loci of TRS, conditioning on their association with general cognitive function, to further elucidate underlying biological mechanisms of TRS.

sec.title: Study design and data sources
sec.p:
In this study, general cognition is represented with general intelligence, years of education and college completion. Publicly available GWAS summary statistics for general intelligence, years of education, college completion, TRS, and schizophrenia were obtained (Fig. 1 for details). All summary statistics were based on individuals of European ancestry. Ethical approval of this study was generated for each of the original GWAS studies. Summary statistics of general intelligence were obtained from 269,867 individuals across 14 cohorts [38]. General intelligence was operationalized as a general intelligence factor (GF) across 13 different cognitive tests among these cohorts. Education attainment was measured as the number of years in education (EAY) [39] and college completion (CC) [40]. Genetic variants association estimates with TRS were obtained from an interaction GWAS meta-analysis in 10501 TRS patients and 20325 non-TRS controls [31]. GWAS data on schizophrenia were obtained from a GWAS meta-analysis [41] which comprised 53386 cases and 77258 controls (individuals without psychotic disorders).Fig. 1An overview of the study design and the included samples.
A The Mendelian randomization (MR) approach was used to explore the relationship between treatment resistance in schizophrenia and g factor (GF) and education attainment (the number of years in education (EAY) and completion of college (CC)). B The conditional false discovery rate (cFDR) approach was used to improve the discovery of genetic variants associated with treatment resistance in schizophrenia after conditioning on GF and EAY. Complex LD regions were excluded before cFDR: (MHC: hg19 location chr 6: 25119106-33854733) and the 8p23.1 (hg19 location chr 8: 7242715-12483982), to avoid potential biases due to complex LD. C The lead SNP lists associated with TRS after conditioning on GF/EAY, were mapped to genes and gene sets using FUMA. D The included samples in the study. *For the GWAS dataset of the phenotype of the number of years in education, the sample sizes used for Mendelian randomization analysis (sample size = 3037499) and conditional false discovery rate analysis (sample size = 766345 (excluding sample from 23andMe)) were different.

sec.title: Mendelian randomization analyses
sec.p: A two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) method was used to estimate the relationships between general cognition (putative exposures) and the risk of treatment resistance (outcome) in schizophrenia using publicly available GWAS datasets as mentioned above. For each MR analysis, all single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with the exposure of interest at a genome-wide significance threshold (P < 5 × 10−8) were selected as potential instrumental variables. These potential instruments were pruned to near independence using a linkage disequilibrium (LD) threshold of r2 < 0.001 for SNPs within 10,000 kilobases of each other. We used the LD reference panel for the European super-population in the 1000 Genomes Project reference dataset, restricted to bi-allelic SNPs with minor allele frequency >0.01. Exposure and outcome GWAS datasets were harmonized to ensure that genetic variant association estimates corresponded to the effect of the same allele. We used predefined allele frequency information from GWASs to clarify and align the orientation of alleles in the exposure and outcome GWAS.

Univariable MR analyses were performed to estimate each of the following causal effects: (i) GF on TRS, (ii) educational attainment (EAY and CC) on TRS. Inverse-variance weighted (IVW) MR [42] with multiplicative random effects was used as the primary analysis method, as it provides the most efficient combination of the variant-specific ratio estimates and accounts for heterogeneity in the causal estimates obtained from individual variants. Mendelian randomization Egger Regression (MR-Egger) [43], Mendelian randomization pleiotropy residual sum and outlier (MR-PRESSO) [44], weighted median [45], and weighted mode [46] were used in sensitivity analyses as they are more robust to the presence of pleiotropy. We also used the Steiger directionality test [47] to orientate the direction of causality between putative exposures and outcome. Multivariable MR analyses were performed utilizing TRS and genetic instruments from both EAY and GF GWASs. Previous studies reported a genetic association between schizophrenia and cognition [48]. Therefore, sensitivity analysis was conducted utilizing schizophrenia as an outcome against the same putative exposures (Fig. 1), as a verification of the analytical approach.

sec.title: Genetic correlation analyses
sec.p: LD score regression was employed to estimate SNP-based heritability (h²) and global genetic correlation (rg) between TRS/schizophrenia and general cognition (GF, EAY, and CC). For case-control phenotypes, SNP-based heritability was calculated on the liability scale. Standard LD scores, derived from the HapMap 3 reference panel and restricted to European populations, were utilized as provided by a previous study [49].

sec.title: Conditional false discovery rate (cFDR)
sec.p: Cross-trait enrichment of SNP associations between TRS and general cognition (GF, CC, EAY) was visualized using stratified Q-Q plots when a significant MR relationship was found. At the level of P < 0.10, P < 0.010, and P < 0.001, the distribution of P values for the primary phenotypic conditioning on the significance of association with the secondary trait is displayed in each Q-Q plot. We applied the conditional false discovery rate (cFDR) method, which can be used to boost power for genomic discovery of the first phenotype by conditioning on a second phenotype [36, 37]. The cFDR process reorders the test statistics for the primary phenotype based on the SNP-associations found in the secondary phenotype. Variants in the primary trait that are also linked to the secondary trait will result in lower cFDR estimations. An FDR level of 0.01 was used to determine the statistical significance of SNPs. For more details about the conditional FDR, see the original publications [36] and the subsequent review [37]. All analyses were performed after excluding SNPs in the extended major histocompatibility complex (MHC; hg19 location chr 6: 25119106–33854733) and the 8p23.1 (hg19 location chr 8: 7242715–12483982) regions to avoid potential biases due to complex LD [50]. As part of the sensitivity analysis, the schizophrenia GWAS (Fig. 1) was also used to apply the stratified Q-Q plot and cFDR analysis.

sec.title: Locus definitions and annotations
sec.p: Independent genetic loci were defined with reference to the FUMA protocol [51]. Briefly, genetic variants were identified as independent SNPs if they had a cFDR<0.01 and were clumped at r2 < 0.6. Among the identified independent SNPs, lead SNPs were identified by further clumping at r2 < 0.1. Candidate SNPs are SNPs that are correlated with any of the independent SNP (r2 ≥ 0.1). The region containing all these candidate SNPs was regarded as a single independent genomic locus. Loci were merged if they were separated by less than 250 kb. All LD information was calculated with information from European ancestry from the 1000 Genome Project (phase III) reference panel [52, 53]. A locus without any physical overlapping with the findings from the original TRS GWAS [32], previous TRS-related cFDR studies [50], or National Human Genome Research Institute–European Bioinformatics Institute GWAS Catalog [54] was considered as novel.

According to a previous study using cFDR method [55], all candidate SNPs with a cFDR value < 0.1 were annotated with Combined Annotation Dependent Depletion [56], RegulomeDB scores [57], and chromatin states [58] by using FUMA. The candidate SNPs were aligned to genes by three gene-mapping strategies: positional mapping based on their physical proximity with a 10-kb window, expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) mapping, and chromatin interaction mapping. We used the GTEx portal (GTEx v8) [59] to query SNPs for known expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) in multiple tissues, the eQTLGen [60] for blood tissue, and the BRAINEAC [61] for various brain tissues. Credible genes were identified as genes mapped by all three mapping strategies. After excluding mapped genes in complex LD regions (MHC, 8p23.1, MAPT, apolipoprotein E regions), we conducted a gene-set analysis to evaluate whether the genes mapped to the shared loci were enriched via FUMA. Multiple comparisons were corrected using Bonferroni correction and a Bonferroni-adjusted p-value threshold of 0.05 was considered as significant.

All the statistical analyses were conducted using R (version 4.3.2) (The R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria) and FUMA (http://fuma.ctglab.nl). ‘TwoSampleMR’ package (https://github.com/MRCIEU/TwoSampleMR), ‘LDlinkR’ package [62], ‘MVMR’ package [63], ‘fcfdr’ package [64], ‘qqman’ package [65], and ‘cfdr.pleio’ package (https://zenodo.org/records/5750318; https://github.com/alexploner/cfdr.pleio/tree/main/R) were used for the data analyses.

sec.title: Mendelian randomization analyses
sec.p:
The results of univariable MR analyses examining the effects of general cognition on TRS or schizophrenia are shown in Table 1. An inverse association between the EAY and TRS across IVW, weighted median, MR-PRESSO, and MR-Egger methods was seen. The results of the main IVW analysis suggested that each Standard deviation (SD) increase in the genetically predicted EAY was related to a 40.3% reduction in odds of TRS (OR = 0.597, 95%CI 0.479–0.743). The MR Steiger directionality test results indicated that this estimate of causal direction was present (P < 0.001). An inverse association between genetically proxied GF scores and TRS using IVW or the MR-PRESSO was found. The results of the main IVW analysis suggested that each SD increase in genetically predicted GF score was related to a 23.0% reduction in odds of treatment resistance in schizophrenia (OR = 0.770, 95%CI 0.609–0.974). The directionality test conducted by MR Steiger confirmed this estimation of potential causal direction (P < 0.001). An inverse association was found between CC and TRS using IVW, with genetically predicted CC (vs non-completion) linked to a 37.4% reduction in odds of treatment resistance in schizophrenia (OR = 0.626, 95% CI 0.407–0.964), and the MR Steiger directionality test confirmed the potential causal direction (P < 0.001).Table 1

A similar inverse association between genetically predicted GF scores and schizophrenia with IVW or MR-PRESSO was found. The results of the main IVW analysis suggested that each SD increase in genetically predicted GF score was related to a 28.2% reduction in the odds of schizophrenia (OR = 0.718, 95%CI 0.598–0.863). The result of the MR Steiger test suggested that this estimate of causal direction was present (P < 0.001). An association was found between CC and schizophrenia using MR-PRESSO, with genetically predicted CC linked to a 0.9% increase in the odds of schizophrenia (OR = 1.009, 95% CI 1.004–1.014), and the MR Steiger directionality test confirmed this potential causal direction (P < 0.001). No significant association between EAY and schizophrenia was seen with all MR methods.

Inverse variance weighted multivariable MR analyses estimating the direct effect of EAY on TRS conditioned on the GF did not show a significant effect (Table 2), and vice versa.Table 2

sec.title: Genetic correlations
sec.p: The genetic correlations between TRS, schizophrenia, and general cognition phenotypes using LD score regression were shown in Table S1. TRS showed negative genetic correlations with CC (rg = −0.706, p = 0.0001) and the GF (rg = −0.416, p = 0.003). Schizophrenia showed a negative genetic correlation with the GF (rg = −0.217, p = 6.42e-27). No significant genetic correlations of EAY with TRS (rg = −1.435, p = 0.342) or schizophrenia (rg = 0.202, p = 0.204) were identified. The heritability of CC was the lowest (h² = 0.017), compared with EAY (h² = 0.309) and GF (h² = 0.183) in Table S1.

sec.title: Genetic overlap between TRS and general cognition
sec.p:
The stratified QQ plots suggest the presence of SNP enrichment for TRS, for SNPs with evidence of association to GF (Fig. 2A). However, the SNP enrichment for TRS as a function of the significance of SNP association with EAY was relatively less pronounced (Fig. 2C). We re-ranked SNPs of TRS conditioning on their correlation with the GF and EAY, taking advantage of this cross-phenotype polygenic enrichment using cFDR analysis. As a parallel analysis, we similarly applied stratified QQ plots to test the presence of SNP enrichment for schizophrenia conditioning on GF and EAY with similar results found (Figure S1A, S1C). In this case, we re-ranked schizophrenia SNPs conditioning on their association with GF and EAY and presented the cFDR Manhattan plots indicating the significant SNPs after conditioning on GF (Figure S1B) and EAY (Figure S1D).Fig. 2Genetic overlap results of TRS and general cognitive function (GF and EAY).
A Stratified QQ plots showing cross-phenotype polygenic enrichment between treatment resistance in schizophrenia (TRS) and g factor (GF). Plotted are the nominal vs empirical −log10 p values (corrected for inflation) for TRS, below the standard genome-wide association study threshold of p < 5.0 × 10−8, as a function of significance of association with GF at the levels of p ≤ 0.1, p ≤ 0.01, and p ≤ 0.001. The dashed lines indicate the null hypothesis. B Common genetic variants associated with TRS after conditioning on GF at conditional False Discovery Rate (cFDR) < 0.01 after doing clumping. Manhattan plot showing the –log10 transformed cFDR values for each SNP on the y-axis and chromosomal positions along the x-axis. The dotted horizontal line represents the threshold for significant shared associations (cFDR < 0.01). C Stratified QQ plots showing cross-phenotype polygenic enrichment between treatment resistance in schizophrenia (TRS) and the number of years in education (EAY). Plotted are the nominal vs empirical −log10 p values (corrected for inflation) for TRS, below the standard genome-wide association study threshold of p < 5.0 × 10−8, as a function of significance of association with EAY at the levels of p ≤ 0.1, p ≤ 0.01, and p ≤ 0.001. The dashed lines indicate the null hypothesis. D Common genetic variants associated with TRS after conditioning on EAY at conditional False Discovery Rate (cFDR) < 0.01 after doing clumping. Manhattan plot showing the –log10 transformed cFDR values for each SNP on the y-axis and chromosomal positions along the x-axis. The dotted horizontal line represents the threshold for significant shared associations (cFDR < 0.01). Independent lead SNPs are encircled in green. Further details for these loci are provided in Table 3.

sec.title: Shared and novel genetic loci (cFDR) between TRS and GF/EAY
sec.p:
The cFDR Manhattan plot indicating the identified significant SNPs of TRS after conditioning on GF is shown in Fig. 2B. At cFDR <0.01, there were 4 novel genomic loci associated with TRS conditioning on GF (Table 3). The cFDR Manhattan plots indicating the identified loci of TRS after conditioning on EAY is shown in Fig. 2D. We identified 2 novel loci associated with TRS after conditioning on EAY (Table 3) at cFDR <0.01. After excluding duplicated loci, 4 novel genomic loci associated with TRS conditioning on general cognition were identified.Table 3

sec.title: Functional annotation, molecular function analysis, and gene-set enrichment analysis
sec.p: All candidate SNPs with a cFDR value <0.1 within loci associated with TRS after conditioning on GF or EAY were functionally annotated, which resulted in 145 candidate SNPs associated with TRS after conditioning on GF and 2 candidate SNPs associated with TRS after conditioning on EAY. After conditioning TRS on the G factor, none of the significant SNPs identified overlapped with those reported for schizophrenia (Table S1-4 in Supplement 2). Among these genomic loci, Combined Annotation Dependent Depletion scores indicated 1 lead SNPs (TRS conditioning on GF: rs538276;) as pathogenic (CADD > 12.37; Table S1 in Supplement 2). By using three different mapping strategies, 113 genes were mapped from candidate SNPs within locus associated with TRS conditioning on GF, 62 genes given the conditional association of EAY, and resulting in 2 credible genes for TRS conditioning on GF, 2 credible genes for TRS conditioning on EAY being found. The only shared credible genes mapped to loci was TMX1 (Table 3). Results of gene expression analyses shown in Figure S2-3 in Supplement 1, indicate that candidate genes for TRS conditioning on GF tend to be upregulated during early prenatal period, while candidate genes for TRS conditioning on EAY tend to be down regulated during early prenatal period, though neither reached statistical significance.

After excluding LD complex regions, mapped genes for TRS conditioning on GF were enriched in 6 GO terms, including “GOCC_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX”, “GOCC_NLRP3_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX”, and “GOMF_CASPASE_BINDING”. Mapped genes for TRS conditioning on EAY were enriched in 8 different gene sets including Psychosis (atypical). All the mapped genes and gene sets are listed in Table S5-8 in Supplement 2. The functional annotation results of all candidate SNPs with a cFDR value < 0.1 within loci associated with schizophrenia after conditioning on GF or EAY were presented in Table S3-4 of Supplement 2.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The MR analysis revealed negative genetic impacts of general cognition on treatment resistance in schizophrenia with a possible causative nature with general cognition represented by education years and G factor. With further cFDR, functional annotation and gene-set enrichment analysis, we identified 4 novel genomic loci associated with TRS after conditioning on general cognition. Notably, one lead SNP of these loci (rs7626676 on chromosome 3) had been previously reported to be associated with schizophrenia. The only shared credible genes mapped to loci was TMX1 (Table 3). Genes associated with TRS conditioning on general cognition were enriched in the GOMF_CASPASE_BINDING gene set, GOCC_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX gene set, GOCC_NLRP3_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX gene set, and GOMF_CYSTEINE_TYPE_ENDOPEPTIDASE_ACTIVITY gene set.

In the current study, general cognition was represented by G factor, education years and college completion and significant relationships were found between G factor, education years, college completion and TRS, and between G factor, college completion and schizophrenia with MR analysis. The genetic relationships between TRS and college completion should be interpreted with caution, as college completion may be more strongly influenced by social factors, may have lower heritability, and may be estimated with limited statistical power due to the small number of genetic instruments in the GWAS. The causative link between general cognition and schizophrenia in the current MR study corroborates with the existing findings of the genetic link between cognition and schizophrenia [50, 66, 67]. We similarly found a causative link between general cognition and treatment resistance in schizophrenia with the same MR approach. The contribution of the G factor to schizophrenia appeared similar to that observed for TRS based on comparable MR IVW effect sizes and negative genetic correlations; however, this similarity does not imply shared genetic variants. In fact, our cFDR analyses did not identify overlap between variants linking the G factor to schizophrenia and those linking it to TRS, suggesting that the comparable effect estimates may arise from distinct underlying genetic architectures. A significant relationship of genetic contribution of education years and TRS was found but not with schizophrenia. Furthermore, each increase of one standard deviation of education years was related to 40.3% of reduction of odds of TRS, much higher than that of the contribution of G factor (23%) to TRS. Apart from cognition, years of education may also be contributed by socioeconomic status, social skills, and academic performance-related psychosocial factors [68], including personality traits, motivational factors, self-regulatory learning strategies, and psychosocial contextual influences [69]. These results echoed the previous finding that premorbid academic domains were associated with cognition outcomes whereas premorbid social functioning was associated with clinical outcomes in schizophrenia specifically the development of treatment resistance [2, 67, 70]. Our results thus suggested the possible shared genetic contribution between schizophrenia and development of treatment resistance as well as the unique underlying genetic mechanism of TRS. The multivariate MR analyses did not find a significant relationship. The presence of the correlation between intelligence and educational attainment [71] suggests potential interaction effects within the same model. Alternatively, an unidentified shared confounder correlated with both general intelligence and educational attainment could diminish their individual significance when included together in a model.

Previous genetic studies on TRS, including one MR study, one cFDR study and one TRS GWAS study have identified genetic links with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obesity, and intestinal infectious diseases [35], emphasizing metabolic pathways in the mechanisms of TRS, including lipid oxidation, and inflammation [35, 51, 72], which supported the “inflammation and oxidative stress” hypothesis [72] of the neurobiological mechanisms of TRS. The cFDR analysis in this study found 4 novel genomic loci associated with TRS conditioning on general cognitive function, only one (rs7626676 chr3: 135619585-135774361) of them was reported to be associated with schizophrenia in Ashkenazi Jews [74]. These results suggest that TRS may have its own heritable genetic burden that partially overlaps with schizophrenia, which explain the inconsistent findings of the previous studies on relationship between polygenic risk score of TRS and non-TRS [27–31]. Furthermore, one credible gene PYGL identified in this study has been shown to support glucose generation to maintain blood glucose levels [73]. These results highlight the possible role of metabolic mechanisms in the TRS development.

The shared credible gene TMX1 and enriched gene sets converge on inflammasome- and caspase-related pathways, including GOCC_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX and GOCC_NLRP3_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX, as well as additional enriched functional gene sets such as GOMF_CASPASE_BINDING, GOMF_CYSTEINE_TYPE_ENDOPEPTIDASE_ACTIVITY, and protease/peptidase inhibitor complexes, suggesting the possible role of inflammatory process and neurodegenerative process in TRS development. TMX1 is a redox-sensitive oxidoreductase located in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) that regulates ER–mitochondria Ca²⁺ flux [75]. ER stress and unfolded protein response (UPR) signalling are key regulators of immune cell function and inflammatory responses [76]. The inflammasome complex, a multi-protein oligomer, is critical for innate immune response and inflammation, primarily activating inflammatory caspases leading to the maturation and secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18 [77]. Our results indicated the mapped genes of TRS were enriched in the GOMF_CASPASE_BINDING, GOCC_NLRP3_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX, and GOCC_INFLAMMASOME_COMPLEX gene sets, which may also be part of the evidence of inflammasome-related inflammatory caspase signalling in TRS. Various brain cell types express different inflammasomes [78–80]: NLRP1 in neurons, NLRP2 in astrocytes, and NLRP3 in microglia, driving neuroinflammatory responses through IL-1β and IL-18 release, resulting in pyroptosis [81]. These findings suggest potential chronic neuroinflammation as biological mechanisms of TRS. Caspases (cysteine-aspartic proteases) are endoproteases with crucial roles in cell regulatory networks controlling inflammation and cell death [82]. Caspase pathway activation was a classical initiation of apoptosis, which plays a prominent role during neurodevelopment as well as in neurodegeneration [83]. Excessive caspase activation and the resultant cell death can contribute to neurodegenerative conditions [84], including Parkinson’s disease and Alzheimer’s disease. The enrichment in the GOMF_CASPASE_BINDING gene set may also support the apoptosis pathway in TRS, paralleling hypotheses that apoptosis may contribute to the pathophysiology of schizophrenia [85].

This study used the only available TRS GWAS, which was largely based on samples who use clozapine. However, due to underutilization of clozapine in the TRS population [86], many patients with TRS are not taking clozapine. Therefore, GWAS data of clozapine users’ only sample may limit its representation of TRS. Confounders and biases may affect genetic study participants. For example, schizophrenia patients able to provide informed consent for genetic studies often have higher education and cognitive ability compared to those in the Clozapine UK cohort (CLOZUK) samples, whose DNA was collected anonymously during routine Clozapine treatment. Besides, the absence of external validation is another key limitation. Without validation through independent cohorts, the findings should be interpreted with caution. Another key limitation of this study is the predominance of individuals of European ancestry in the GWAS samples. Although the cFDR approach was utilized to leverage genetic overlap between TRS and GF/EAY, identifying the causal variants underlying the overlapping genomic loci remains challenging due to the cross-trait enrichment nature of the cFDR method. More diverse and larger treatment resistant schizophrenia samples, alongside new trans-ancestral methods and analyses are needed to enhance the generalizability of these findings. Finally, the general latent factor for intelligence used in the study may not precisely correspond to IQ scores, and it is possible that factors other than cognition could have influenced the year in education or college completion.

In conclusion, the current study provides preliminary evidence on the possible causative link between general cognition or a closely correlated trait and treatment resistant schizophrenia. We identified 4 previously unreported genomic loci associated with TRS by leveraging the genetic overlap with general cognition, most likely to be related to neurodegenerative and inflammatory processes. These findings suggest a complex and unique biological mechanism in the development of treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS). Results may contribute to further understanding of the mechanism of TRS and exploration of effective interventional strategies for patients with treatment-resistant schizophrenia.

sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41398-026-03994-8.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors thank the research participants, employees, and researchers of the PGC, SSGAC, and other related GWAS datasets for making this research possible. We also thank Alexey A. Shadrin and their team for their help on the Methods part of this study, thank Ploner Alexander and their team for building and sharing “cfdr.pleio” r package, and thank Antonio F Pardiñas and their team for sharing the TRS GWAS database and clarifying the sample.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: PCS and SC designed the study. CL performed the analyses, interpreted the results, wrote the manuscript; PCS and SC supervised the study, revised the study design, interpreted the results, revised the manuscript; and YZ helped perform and interpret the results. All authors reviewed the study findings and read and approved the final version before submission.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Data supporting the findings of this study are openly available from an online repository or are available on request from study authors

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The funding institutions of this study had no further role in the study design, the collection, analysis and interpretation of data, the writing of this paper, or the decision to submit for publication. The authors report no financial relationships with commercial interests.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: All methods were performed in accordance with the relevant guidelines and regulations. This study is based on publicly available GWAS summary statistics. Ethical approval and informed consent were obtained in the original studies, as detailed in the referenced publications. No additional ethical approval was required for this secondary analysis.
